item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Now if only that trick could be applied to every instance of redaction on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Things I'm not super a fan of:
-Morse code trope. Also the whole diary of the person figuring out the Morse code/hexadecimal thing felt pointless.
- The BBC report felt unrealistic. If you meant for it to read like they were panicking, that didn't work. It just read as silly.
- The ending taking so much work to figure out.

Things I did like:
- Basically all of the multiversal contact and timeline shenanigans. We really don't get enough of this stuff. The table was also very cool.

All in all, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On Linux systems, you can set a flag on files that let you execute them like that, as long as you have “#!/usr/bin/python” at the top

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
knewmans@5034_s19: ./hexconvert.py -i transmission.dat -o hexnums.txt

Shouldn't Python script be run through Python interpreter as:

knewmans@5034_s19: python ./hexconvert.py -i transmission.dat -o hexnums.txt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny thing is, even with the TEAM CA3-O5 no longer present in the document, the morse code still mentions it when "translated" to proper text.
UPD: The morse code is even weirder. The bytes are grouped in pairs with their order reversed, so instead of ABCDEFGH you get BADCFEHG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As confusing as it my be to some people, I myself find the trick changing the names of the archives the most interesting screw in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is what the hex string says

+ show block
– hide block

TO WHOM IT MAY CONCERN - THIS IS AN AUTOMATED ME0SSAGE BROADCAST @BY THE SCP FOUNDPATION UNIVERSE 2`a0e8a129b SCRANTpON REALITY ANCHO�R - IF YOU ARE R�ECEIVING THIS ME�SSAGE THIS UNIVE�RSE IS IN DANGER�- PLEASE EVACUAT�E AS FAST AS POS�SIBLE TO LOWER C�HANCE OF COLLAPSATION EVENT - THANK YOU — FROM PARAGON
(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was one heck of a fun read, and was VERY tied into the history of SCP. I might have to come back to this once I'm savvier. But until then…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think expecting the reader to "figure out the trick" of using lvl5 or using a hex reader is a step too far, in all honesty.

Ultimately, it's a question of how far you want the reader to make the effort necessary to receive your story, though. With the note on the author's post, it's not too hard, and I appreciate that. I would never have used a hex reader (nor would I feel happy with a skip that specifically requires one).

I do, however, appreciate the Prince of Darkness vibe of the message, and despite the message itself being something I could expect, I still enjoyed the story overall. +1.

(Also for anyone who hasn't seen Prince of Darkness, it's incredible SCP fuel. It's definitely one the main inspirations for my work I've had over the years, and it's been almost 30 years since I've seen it in total.)

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
4kon has closed out, and I ended with 24th place. Honestly, I'm happy with that. If I was the 24th best writer on the site, I'd be ecstatic.

I've claimed the number of SCP-4800, and along with the number switch, I've made some science fixes (courtesy of Voct) and adjusted some of the fine details to set up for a series I'll be laying out as Series V goes along.

Congrats to all of the people who participated in this contest, and thank you to all of the readers who made it possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done. Felt rewarding. I really liked the code tricks.

FWIW, I immediately picked up on changing the filename manually.

I was originally going to do a Yeti for the 4000. But I didn't, because I got the better idea of a world where the Revolutionary War never ended. But after I realized that I didn't have the writing dexterity to pull that off, I decided to use this idea. So glad I did.

(Side note: I might turn these ideas into skips later on. So don't steel plz)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for any skip that includes a Unix shell in the logs, but this one is awesome even besides that. I've long thought we needed more exploration of what SRAs do to the universes they slurp reality juice from, and this does a great job of doing that.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I really appreciated the way it tied into the theme of world history and site history at the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good Shit. +1

(I may also have had too much fun playing around with the search-box at the end.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this article! It both intriguing, truly frightening, AND adds some really nice conceptual ideas to the whole merging timelines business. Cosmic horror at its finest!

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Death of the Author and all, plus I’m probably going to use the Multiversal Foundation Alliance and the Paragon universe in future skips/tales and I don’t want to play all of my cards too quickly. :-)

However, the last two interpretations are the closest to what’s going on.

(Also, I’ve been reading the Hyperion series lately. Nice reference.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I still don't get it...
– hide block

Do they just happen to be merging with the CA3-O5 timeline, and they are able to communicate through the wormhole at the end and give them a (late and useless) warning…

Or, they're merging with some other timeline, and CA3-O5 timeline knows how to communicate with them and give them a (late and useless) warning…

Or, CA3-O5 are triggering the merges (either with their own timeline(s), or just between other timelines but maybe with the intent of eventually merging with their own) to consolidate all containment actions across the multiverse under themselves.

Any answer? :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Replies to Nitpicks
- digiorno text

This seems contradictory; it can't be observed, but we can totally observe it from the outside.

Fair; I've changed it to say you can't observe the inner process.

Presumably if the timelines are merged, there will be lots of evidence of the previous history.

I just axed that line. I was never happy with it.

Does this mean that affected timelines functionally lag behind the rest of the multiverse? For example, is baseline time 23 years behind the alternate Foundation that contacted it?

Yes. I've added a line to make it more specific.

By "the timeline", do you mean baseline Foundation's timeline?

Yes; I've made that clearer.

The internet would work just fine without satellites; it's not reliant on satellites to function at all, that's what all the under-sea cables are for. You bring up this whole thing with the internet going down a lot but it's not accurate. Some services might degrade or completely break as clocks begin to go out of sync but the internet itself would keep working.

I've added a sentence or two in the Description to help with that.

I find this pretty unlikely. Modern satellites are designed to completely burn up on re-entry to prevent this exact thing, and even older ones would mostly burn up. Certainly there wouldn't be enough left upon impact to cause this level of damage.

Changed this to a meteor.

The entries in this diary are dates 2020; is the filename not mean to be a date?

It was supposed to be an ID number; changed 2034 to 5034 to hopefully prevent this mistake.

The "from earlier" bit here reads very fourth-wall breaking.

Axed that bit.

I didn't pick up on the changing filename to access unredacted version of the files thing, so I thought I had to translate the hex myself, a level of hoop-jumping I actively resent having to perform to understand an article. I'd suggest some other, more clear way for readers to get to that ending.

Honestly, I'm this close to just redacting the hex alongside the words just to prevent people from having this issue. I want thoughts on it before I do it, though.

I believe the good majority of the issues you pointed out have been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright so this is cool, but I have many questions/issues from places that I found to be contradictory or insufficiently explained.

Stuff!
– hide block

Due to its nature, the process of SCP-4000 has not been directly observed. However, SCP-4000 can be observed from the outside, and proceeds as follows:

This seems contradictory; it can't be observed, but we can totally observe it from the outside.

SCP-4000-A1 will reopen. There will be no evidence of SCP-4000-A1's previous history.

Presumably if the timelines are merged, there will be lots of evidence of the previous history.

No evidence of SCP-4000-related phenomena have been preserved; it is believed that SCP-4000 reverses the timeline to the beginning of the phenomena.

Does this mean that affected timelines functionally lag behind the rest of the multiverse? For example, is baseline time 23 years behind the alternate Foundation that contacted it?

SCPF-7a5f7b had been observing the timeline's history

By "the timeline", do you mean baseline Foundation's timeline?

Yes, and it's also why the internet's down. We can't get to the satellites because they're outside of Earth's atmosphere.

The internet would work just fine without satellites; it's not reliant on satellites to function at all, that's what all the under-sea cables are for. You bring up this whole thing with the internet going down a lot but it's not accurate. Some services might degrade or completely break as clocks begin to go out of sync but the internet itself would keep working.

A satellite crashes to Earth, causing high levels of damage in Lower Manhattan. The World Trade Center is destroyed as a result.

I find this pretty unlikely. Modern satellites are designed to completely burn up on re-entry to prevent this exact thing, and even older ones would mostly burn up. Certainly there wouldn't be enough left upon impact to cause this level of damage.

Date: 05/12/2017
Description: Primarily due to a lack of communication between world governments and the civilian populace about the SCP-4000 event, riots begin to occur worldwide. In most areas, government control is held.

This took a year before it happened?!

diary_2034_katie.rtf

The entries in this diary are dates 2020; is the filename not mean to be a date?

The dissolution field from earlier is starting to

The "from earlier" bit here reads very fourth-wall breaking.

I didn't pick up on the changing filename to access unredacted version of the files thing, so I thought I had to translate the hex myself, a level of hoop-jumping I actively resent having to perform to understand an article. I'd suggest some other, more clear way for readers to get to that ending.

Also, if there's an underlying theme to the new events introduced by these merging timelines, I'm not seeing it.

So yeah, lots of very specific or nit-picky stuff there, but I did actually like this article. If some of these issues were fixed, I'd definitely upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting.

While the base idea of merging and collapsing timelines is not a new idea, the approach here is admirable and the supplementary material provides some solid world building for a version of the SCP Multiverse where these events occur commonly.

The trick with upping the credentials to remove the redactions was also rather clever.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it turns out that xxd -r can read hexdump formatted dumps. the whole step where i manually changed it to the xxd format was a waste of effort, oops

(of course, SSHing into my production server in the first place was a waste of effort when i could have just looked at the new filename. oh well, it was fun :3)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
undergroundmonorail decoded the hex from the document manually. I thought this was kinda cool, so I'm posting it here.

Spoilers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow, this was incredible. the formatting was great, the implications were great, overall an excellent skip in my opinion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahahahah no problem! Credit where credit is due.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Consider it an apology for delaying the rewrite for the millionth time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why did seagull use my last name in a cameo lol

upvoted purely for that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Significantly improved from the draft that I read. An interesting anomaly, a great little mystery at the end, and an old-school "oh SHIT" moment to top it all off. Marvelous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate that there's like, probably one other person on this site who would think of this, but I looked at the description and went, "It's Oblivaeon" and had a hard time taking the whole thing seriously. Not your fault, but "I couldn't grok this because it reminds me of something no one but me has ever heard of" is a common occurrence around here.

Still, I like the coding done for that final addendum, though the event log is goofy, what with all the redaction. And I really don't get what was going on with the numerical code. opaque, and I had to read the spoilers to figure it out. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew this was yours while reading just because no one else can do fancy code tricks quite like you, that 4-5 file lookup was very clever. Overall, an ambitious idea that's executed well without ever making itself too obvious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh shit

spoilers
shall not pass

i is very confused rite know.

So, I'm assuming that SCP-001 Scranton was warning Timeline E-2a0e8a129b before the collapse, but obviously the 001 Scranton universe Foundation is dumb as fuck so of course the message is encrypted. The Timeline E-2a0e8a129b Foundation does not decode this in time, as Newmans is seen attempting to communicate to other Sites, only to find out no one is there to respond. He realises all hope is lost, but transfers to information to the DEEPWELL, and classifies the info as LEVEL 5 in order to warn other universes.

OR… Kate really did find a way to stop or even use SCP-4000. Otherwise, why would the LTG Foundation Alliance still attempt to contact Timeline E-2a0e8a129b? Unkess they know it didn't die! Maybe there is a document not even included in the list of files that explains more. If Kate didn't stop SCP-4000, can that universe even be called Timeline E-2a0e8a129b anymore?

GASP. That was long. Give me a minute to catch my breath.

So, I probably got it all wrong. If so, I guess I'm a fool.

Anyways, great skip. One of my favourites, even if it's not actually got a number yet. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I read this for a draft trade, and dang there's a lot here. Typically not my sort of article, but I can get behind the neat supplementary material and the implications. Definitely a "makes you think" piece.

+1 from me here, though I'll likely need to return to this for a close read when I have the time (I typically read really fast…) in order to get the max enjoyment for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I decided to leave it in as a way for the people who couldn’t figure out the lvl5 thing to decode the document. However, it wouldn’t be too much trouble to censor the hexadecimal part too. Tell me what you think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: + Spoilers not really relevant anymore

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, and I also really like how the timeline of observed divergences tie back to the history of the wiki itself.

Spoilers ahead
– hide block

So, if I understood the ending correctly it is implied that Scantron reality anchors are actually causing the timeline collapses and that judging by the automated message at least some people at the Foundation (at least one of them anyway) knows about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sorta like the format. Good luck! I'll try to compete somehow.

By the way, in the News_Report.pdf, Sprinkler says: "aive", instead of alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one thing I'll say is that it was very annoying to C+V all the hexadecimal numbers into a converter so I could figure out what the last message said.

Also, this seems like one of the more bizarre expungements I've seen - we'll give you the code, make it obvious how to translate it, and then expunge what it says. It's like having EXPUNGING a translation but leaving the original translation up and clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You cleaned this up pretty nicely from when I read it— one thing I didn't catch until just now was that you refer to the timeline as "E-a08…" in the procedures and "2-a08" in the actual unredacted document.

Still, hearty +1. Good luck!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Here it is. This is probably the most ambitious skip I've written. I don't even want the 4000 slot; I just really want the 4800 slot1. There's also HTML here, so if you have NoScript or something on it might not show up.

A lot of people need to be thanked for this:

  • DrChandra, for taking a look at an early draft and telling me it was a good idea
  • weizhong and Tat0b910, for helping me flesh out my Description and making my skip overall more interesting
  • GerrymanderBassist, SeptemberJackal does not match any existing user name, LordStonefish, XilasCrowe, Gabriel Jade and Ihp, for proofreading this at various stages throughout the draft.
+ In case you don't figure it out...
– hide block

For the file names that end in "lvl4", you can change "4" to "5" to make the file unredacted.

Pictures:

  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Destroyed_building_in_Muzaffarabad_after_the_earthquake_(2005).jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Acentronura_tentaculata,_Dahab.jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Medvedev_and_Vladimir_Yakunin.jpg

Edit: I'd like to bring attention to a French translation by DrGemini

Footnotes
1. 48 is my favorite number :-)
